name: Discount Impact on Margin
description: What a percentage discount does to price and margin, and the extra volume needed to compensate: the discounted price, the new gross margin, and the percentage uplift in units required to keep the same gross profit as before the discount.

inputs.name: Selling price
inputs.description: Regular net selling price per unit, before the discount.

inputs.name: Unit cost
inputs.description: Cost of goods per unit; assumed unchanged by the discount.

inputs.name: Discount
inputs.description: Discount as a percentage of the selling price, e.g. 10 for 10% off.

outputs.name: New gross margin
outputs.description: Gross margin at the discounted price.

outputs.name: Required volume uplift
outputs.description: Percentage more units you must sell at the discounted price to earn the same gross profit as before.

expression: P_d = P x (1 - disc/100); new_margin_pct = 100 x (P_d - C) / P_d; uplift_pct = 100 x ((P - C) / (P_d - C) - 1)
assumptions: Unit cost is constant — the discount changes price only, not cost of goods.
The uplift compares gross profit per unit before and after; it says nothing about whether the discount will actually generate that extra volume.
The discount applies to every unit sold, not just incremental units.
limitations: Requires P_d > C — a discount deeper than the margin makes every sale loss-making, and no volume uplift can recover the gross profit.
Ignores second-order effects: cannibalisation, customers trained to wait for discounts, and fulfilment costs that scale with the extra volume.
A gross-profit view; contribution after variable selling costs may need a larger uplift still.

examples.note: A 10% discount on a 40%-margin item drops the price to 90 and the margin to 33.3% — and requires 33.3% more units sold (40/30 per-unit profit ratio) just to hold the same gross profit.

sources: Standard gross-margin arithmetic; discount break-even (iso-profit volume) analysis
